channel.title: Whamcloud Community JIRA
channel.description: This file is an XML representation of an issue

item.title: [LU-17431] dynamically configurable nodemap
item.description:
A number of sites using containers with Lustre have requested the ability to dynamically configure nodemaps on a regular basis to handle environments where they are being created and removed on a e.g. a per-job basis to contain only the nodes in the job for a specific runtime environment.

Storing all of the nodemaps in the config llog with frequent updates would quickly consume all of the space in the config log, and be slow to process during mounting.

Instead, there should be some option to allow using "lctl set_param nodemap.NODEMAP.PARAM" or an option to "lctl nodemap" to directly create and manage nodemaps without using the config llog to distribute the parameters to each server. This would require some external orchestration to set the nodemap parameters consistently across servers (e.g. "clush -a lctl set_param ...").

It might be desirable to have these dynamic nodemaps inherit settings from an existing persistent nodemap that would otherwise catch their NID range. This would have a number of benefits:

  • safety in case of server restart so that missing dynamic nodemaps do not result in clients in the default nodemap
  • avoid the need to set every parameter for the new nodemap, only change the subdirectory and NIDs and possibly a few other parameters
  • (optionally?) make the dynamic nodemaps "safe by default" in that they can only add further restrictions rather than weaken them (e.g. longer subdirectory path, only disable access permissions, only remove UID mappings from the parent). Not totally sure about this, but seems interesting.

For identifying at mount time which nodemap is used, this could be hierarchical. Find the top-level NID range as today and map it to a nodemap, and consider this the "parent" nodemap. Then check if there is a second-level NID range below the parent and re-scan for matching NIDs and add extra restrictions from the child nodemap. Possibly repeat (max 10 levels for safety?).

That avoids making the initial NID searching complex (broad NID ranges can be used for the whole cluster) and then become more finegrained only if needed.

status.description: A resolution has been taken, and it is awaiting verification by reporter. From here issues are either reopened, or are closed.

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54513/
Subject: LU-17431 nodemap: allow modifying properties of a dynamic nm
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54509/
Subject: LU-17431 nodemap: allow handling an id map for a dynamic nm
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:
Commit: fb3459589e98169b1af2858abc70fafdf59c313d

sebastien, adilger,

I'm currently reviewing Sébastien's patches on this ticket, and I've two questions regarding the current patch series:

1. Is there any update on your previous comments on whether hierarchical nodemaps must be dynamic and in-memory only? It seems to me that hierarchical nodemaps can also be persistently stored on the nodemap IAM and do not need to be dynamic (temporary). The restriction would be that persistent hierarchical nodemaps must be created on the MGS while dynamic nodemaps can be temporarily created on the MDS/OSS.

On another note, if persistent hierarchical nodemaps would be beneficial, it could make sense to change the terminology a bit (as Sébastien hinted): Since hierarchical nodemaps do not need to be dynamic in this case, they do not need a special name or property as they "only" inherit the settings of the parent. "Dynamic" nodemaps could be called "temporary" nodemaps to be more descriptive and to indicate that they are not persistent.

2. There is also the question of how hierarchical nodemaps should work with multiple filesets. The proposed idea in #55614 is that a child fileset must be a subdirectory of the parent fileset, and thus doesn't weaken the namespace restrictions set by the parent nodemap. The same can be done for multiple filesets such that child filesets must always have an existing parent fileset in the parent nodemap and represent a subdir of it. Is this correct?

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54508/
Subject: LU-17431 nodemap: allow handling a nidrange for a dynamic nm
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:
Commit: 257d6f392777fbf76e82304f5bfd61a75db1c76c

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54507/
Subject: LU-17431 nodemap: allow add/del of a dynamic nodemap
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:
Commit: abaa91c12c9bc89a88fa4607accd27aa31900cce

This is interesting. I haven't checked myself, but that is good news if adding and removing nodemaps does not consume space in the CONFIGS/nodemap file. I guess this is one of the problems we wanted to address with dynamic nodemaps though. The other issue with legacy nodemaps is the time to propagate modifications from the MGS to the MDS/OSS servers. When people want to create nodemaps for the duration of a job (and then remove once the job is over), it can be painful to wait for the nodemap definitions to be updated. In this case, dynamic nodemaps can make the process much more fluent. So are you suggesting we should keep the idea of dynamic nodemaps, i.e. nodemaps that are created directly on MDS/OSS side, but make them persistent on disk? In this case maybe they should be named differently, although 'dynamic' does not necessarily mean 'in-memory only'.

Sebastien,
this may be a dumb question, but I'm wondering whether dynamic (non-persistent) nodemaps even necessary? I had previously thought that the nodemap code was using a configuration llog to store the nodemap information, which would use fixed-size records.

However, after working on the nodemap offset patch I now understand that the nodemap configuration records are stored in an OSD index object (IAM on ldiskfs, ZAP on ZFS, like the OI files on the MDT), and these are dynamically sized/managed and can grow quite large without running out of space. Also, the OSD index objects have the added benefit that deleted entries in the index free up space that can be re-used when new entries are added to the index. While this is not necessarily perfect, since it depends on the hash distribution and how the leaf buckets are split, the index can definitely withstand a large number of insert/delete cycles. The same OSD index objects are used to store hundreds of millions of FID->inode mappings.

If there are not similar limitations on the OSD index as there are in the llog files for continually adding and removing nodemap config records, then it isn't clear if we the dynamic nodemaps should only be stored in memory, but should instead always be stored on disk? That avoids one of the major issues with dynamic nodemaps - namely that they are lost if the MDS crashes.

I did a quick experiment and added 1000 nodemaps to a filesystem, deleted them all, and then added 1000 different nodemaps to the filesystem. For both cases, the CONFIGS/nodemap file was about 64KB (slightly larger in the second case), so I think this lends support to the idea that the persistent nodemap config is not as limited as we thought.

At this point, the main scaling limitation appears to be from saving the pathname for the fileset into the "params" config llog used by "lctl set_param -P". That definitely does have cycle limitations, though we could wonder whether the fileset name would change very frequently, or mostly only the NIDs assigned to that nodemap?

Subject: LU-17431 nodemap: fileset inheritance for dynamic nodemap
Project: fs/lustre-release

Commit: 222e90e6b895e439e24a1fb5522bd08aaf71965b

Subject: LU-17431 utils: add 'dynamic' parameter to nodemap_cmd
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:
Commit: fecc3bd4e2042e3b0cd701b3b2a6b3bce21acad6

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54506/
Subject: LU-17431 nodemap: add function to know if nodemap is on MGS
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:
Commit: c5d08e0af6ce819b818a0d9917a41d99899bab67

"Sebastien Buisson <sbuisson@ddn.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54928
Subject: LU-17431 nodemap: do not use copy_from_user for kernel data
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: ab45b17049829af9b56f99fa89eabfd011ca28a1

I don't see it anywhere in your patch series yet, but what are your thoughts about subdirectory mount inheritance for dynamic nodemaps? My thinking is that if a subdirectory mount is specified for a dynamic nodemap that it is appended to any subdirectory mount specified in the static parent nodemap. This would allow configuring e.g. parent = "/projectA", and the dynamic nodemap has "/userB" and the clients in the dynamic nodemap are mounting "/projectA/userB".

We would prevent clients from mounting the "/projectA" nodemap by requiring a "projectA admin" key (SSK/KRB), but the dynamic nodemap nodemap would allow a second "userB" key to mount the filesystem (possibly in addition to "projectA admin"). If it isn't yet possible to register multiple keys for a single nodemap, then this would (IMHO) also be a useful feature, to allow key expiry and transition to a new key without having a "flag day" where all of the clients need to be converted over to the new key. This was requested under LU-8229, but it was never closed or updated, so I don't know if this feature exists today or not.

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54505/

Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 8d402302d11568c31b5698cedd19d023d707fa89

"Sebastien Buisson <sbuisson@ddn.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54735
Subject: LU-17431 nodemap: introduce helper function __range_insert()
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 623ce0df80e793cd6cf97d0e68a35dcdfb92abf3

"Sebastien Buisson <sbuisson@ddn.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54725
Subject: LU-17431 nodemap: forbid dynamic changes of on-disk nodemap
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 63f5dd6b49ab1000f44ba8892e7f3ab58509bbde

Yes, I was thinking that the dynamic nodemaps would be hung off of the parent rather than from the primary RB tree. That allows all nodemap lookups to "pass through" the parent nodemap and then do a subsidiary nodemap lookup for the dynamic ranges, if available.

There was also a request to have an llapi interface to manage nodemaps.

I have pushed first patches to implement dynamic nodemaps. I have divided into multiple small patches to make reviews easy. For now it does not handle the hierarchical nodemaps, it just allows to create and define properties directly on MDS and OSS nodes, and take these dynamic nodemaps into account when clients are connecting.

One limitation I have identified so far is that dynamic nodemaps are lost when any nodemap change or command is done on the MGS. This is because when a nodemap is modified on MGS side, it forces the other servers to refresh the entire nodemap config. After this refresh, it seems complicated to keep the dynamic nodemaps (i.e. automatically without re-running all the 'lctl nodemap_' commands on MDS/OSS side). By definition, the dynamic nodemaps are sitting on top of the legacy nodemaps, so if they change the dynamic nodemaps need to be rebuilt.

I now need to figure out how to implement the NID range hierarchy for the dynamic nodemaps. The NID ranges are currently managed as a red-black tree, so we cannot really tamper with that. But there is a single tree today, with a common root, so maybe it would be possible to implement the sub nid ranges as new r-b trees, attached to the nid range they are a subset of (their parent range).

"Sebastien Buisson <sbuisson@ddn.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54517
Subject: LU-17431 nodemap: allow nodemap_test_id on non-MGS nodes
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 99513be44dbb7eaa5994214a4b32318ee9a695b0

Subject: LU-17431 utils: fix nodemap_cmd ret code
Project: fs/lustre-release

Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 1004a3545f3f0bda9ab0f37658f93e460d6a6703

Since this would only be limiting a nodemap further, then one option that might be acceptable (security wise) is to allow a client with admin/root privs to be able to execute this and have it apply to the servers? Of course, that would add complexity and being able to run a command directly on the servers would be easier for us (if more complex for the user). We'd have to ask one of the sites about this, maybe the author of the LUG (LAD?) presentation that was discussing their complex nodemap config a couple of years ago?

By "repeat" I mean "recurse", if there are multiple levels of sub-maps. For example, a persistent nodemap for 192.168.[0-255].[0-255], with a sub-nodemap covering 192.168.2.[0-255], and a sub-sub-nodemap covering 192.168.2.[16-31]. The matching would be fast at connect time, since the sub-nodemaps would form a tree and reduce the search space at each level.
[...]
Correct, there would only be one pass at each level, and the list of ranges would be disjoint below each nodemap.

OK, I had not thought about hierarchical nodemaps. I guess it can be implemented like that, but it will introduce a little bit more complexity in the code, and in the way to access those sub-nodemaps via lctl.

From an operational perspective, the difficulty I can see is the job scheduler needs to be able to run 'lctl' commands on Lustre server nodes. But maybe this is something usual?

What I mean is that we are talking about special nodemaps that are created 'on-the-fly'. So the job scheduler, or something closely related to the jobs being run on the cluster, would have to be able to create those dynamic nodemaps. And because this is a server side thing, the job scheduler would have to launch lctl commands on the Lustre servers.

I understand this matching procedure, except for the 'Possibly repeat' part. Today we do not support overlapping NID ranges,

By "repeat" I mean "recurse", if there are multiple levels of sub-maps. For example, a persistent nodemap for 192.168.[0-255].[0-255], with a sub-nodemap covering 192.168.2.[0-255], and a sub-sub-nodemap covering 192.168.2.[16-31]. The matching would be fast at connect time, since the sub-nodemaps would form a tree and reduce the search space at each level.

I guess after the legacy NID search, there would be only one pass to find a match in the 'clientmap' ranges?

Correct, there would only be one pass at each level, and the list of ranges would be disjoint below each nodemap.

From an operational perspective, the difficulty I can see is the job scheduler needs to be able to run 'lctl' commands on Lustre server nodes. But maybe this is something usual?

Sorry, I don't understand the question. Just like regular nodemaps this would only be evaluated at connection time to see which top-level and (optionally) sub-nodemaps to get additional settings.

I am not sure how we could name this feature, maybe 'clientmap' so that it clearly differs from the persistent nodemap (and it targets client nodes, if I understand correctly the customer requirements)?

To my thinking "dynamic nodemaps" or "inherited nodemaps" is fine, since the majority of the functionality is identical to regular nodemaps except they aren't persistent, and they are hierarchical from the parent nodemaps.

Hi Andreas,

If I understand correctly, that would be an in-memory only, non persistent nodemap feature that overrides the 'legacy' nodemap feature, in a sense that it would only modify a subset of all nodemap properties available. Because of the 'default' legacy nodemap, there is always a matching nodemap for clients. So that would not be a problem to make these dynamic nodemaps inherit settings from an existing persistent nodemap. I am not sure how we could name this feature, maybe 'clientmap' so that it clearly differs from the persistent nodemap (and it targets client nodes, if I understand correctly the customer requirements)?

From an implementation point of view, I think this makes sense. It would reuse some of the already defined data structures for nodemap, but not modify the 'legacy' nodemap feature itself.

For identifying at mount time which nodemap is used, this could be hierarchical. Find the top-level NID range as today and map it to a nodemap, and consider this the "parent" nodemap. Then check if there is a second-level NID range below the parent and re-scan for matching NIDs and add extra restrictions from the child nodemap. Possibly repeat (max 10 levels for safety?).

I understand this matching procedure, except for the 'Possibly repeat' part. Today we do not support overlapping NID ranges, so I guess after the legacy NID search, there would be only one pass to find a match in the 'clientmap' ranges?

From an operational perspective, the difficulty I can see is the job scheduler needs to be able to run 'lctl' commands on Lustre server nodes. But maybe this is something usual?

Hello Andreas,

yes, that makes sense.
It would also apply to SLURM jobs where you would want SLURM to create a nodemap per job containing the NIDs assigned to the job by SLURM.

sebastien, bolausson,
any thoughts on this proposal? Does this match what you would expect from functionality and the ability to implement this in a reasonable manner?
